paragraphs: Native Americans have inhabited the area called Acadia for at least 12,000 years, including the coastal areas of Maine, Canada, and adjacent islands. The Wabanaki Confederacy ("People of the Dawnland") consists of five related Algonquian nations—the Maliseet, Mi'kmaq, Passamaquoddy, Abenaki and Penobscot. Some of the nations call Mount Desert Island Pemetic ("range of mountains"), which has remained at the center of the Wabanaki traditional ancestral homeland and territory of traditional stewardship responsibility to the present day. The etymology of the park's name begins with the Mi'kmaq term akadie ("piece of land") which was rendered as l'Acadie by French explorers, and translated into English as Acadia.

The Wabanaki traveled to the island in birch bark canoes to hunt, fish, gather berries, harvest clams and basket-making resources like sweetgrass, and to trade with other Wabanakis. They camped near places like Somes Sound.

In the early 17th century, Asticou was the chieftain of the greater Mount Desert Island area, one district of an intertribal confederacy known as Mawooshen led by the grandchief Bashaba. Castine (Pentagoet in the native language) was the grandchief's favored rendezvous site for the Wabanaki tribes. The site is located just west of Mount Desert Island at the mouth of the Bagaduce River in eastern Penobscot Bay. From 1615, Castine developed into a major fur trading post where French, English, and Dutch traders all fought for control. Sealskins, moose hides, and furs were traded by the Wabanakis for European commodities. By the early 1620s, warfare and introduced diseases, including smallpox, cholera and influenza, had decimated the tribes from Mount Desert Island southward to Cape Cod, leaving about 10 percent of the original population.

The border established between the United States and Canada after the American Revolution split the Wabanaki homelands. The confederacy was dissolved around 1870 due to pressure from the American and Canadian governments, though the tribal nations continued to interact in their traditional ways. In the nineteenth century, Wabanakis sold handmade ash and birch bark baskets to travelers. The Wabanaki performed dances for summer tourists and residents at Sieur de Monts and the town of Bar Harbor. Wabanaki guides led canoe trips around Frenchman Bay and the Cranberry Islands.

gallery.cap: Otter Cliffs Radio Station wayside exhibit
gallery.desc: National Park Service wayside exhibit about the Otter Cliffs Radio Station, located in the Fabbri Picnic Area of Acadia National Park.
Clips featuring people of all ages hiking, enjoying scenery, kayaking and canoeing, and taking photos against scenic backdrops.

gallery.cap: Acadia B Roll Peaks and Scenery (53942874226)
gallery.desc: A series of clips featuring mountains, forests, and water viewed from above.
Clips showing coastlines, parking lots, and urban areas that have large crowds of people and lines.

Acadia National Park, Sieur de Monts. Thanks for the Explore! This juvenile porcupine was on the side of a carriage road in the park, just eating. Another group of hikers had seen it before me. Then, a bigger group of tourists came by, and a ranger asked from afar, "Is that what I think it is?" I nodded "Yes", but now I think she was probably asking whether it was a bear.
Wide shot of the schooner Margaret Todd in the Mt. Desert Narrows, Bar Harbor, ME. August 2015.
Bar Island, an uninhabited tidal island in Bar Harbor, Maine. It's part of the Acadia National Park.

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road A Bar Harbor Church in the Fall
gallery.desc: Leafless trees frame this photo of a white church in Bar Harbor. Location: Maine (44.388° N 68.206° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by Dennis Adams

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road Acadia National Park Architecture
gallery.desc: Fine landscaping surrounds a carriage road house in Acadia National Park. Location: Acadia National Park, Maine (44.342° N 68.273° W) Status: Public domain. Photos taken by Bill Compton

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road Anemone Cave
gallery.desc: This cave is accessible at low tide where the Schooner Head Overlook trail meets the Atlantic Ocean. There is a pink tidal pool inside where the sea anemones live. Location: Anemone Cave, Schooner Head Overlook, Maine (44.322° N 68.314° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by Leonard Kharlen

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road Bar Harbor Bus System
gallery.desc: Learn where to catch the Bar Harbor bus by studying this sign. Location: Maine (44.388° N 68.205° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by Dennis Adams

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road Street in Bar Harbor
gallery.desc: Tourists seeking color fill the streets during the fall in Bar Harbor. Location: Maine (44.389° N 68.208° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by Dennis Adams

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road The Bubbles on Jordan Pond
gallery.desc: Blue waters contrast against fall hills at the Bubbles on Jordan Pond. Location: Maine (44.334° N 68.256° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by Dennis Adams

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road Thompson Island Information Center
gallery.desc: The Thompson Island Information Center is a cluster of small, weathered wood structures with an interpretive sign in front. Location: Thompson Island, Maine (44.427° N 68.366° W) Status: Public domain.

gallery.cap: Acadia All American Road Visitors at an Acadia Byway Overlook
gallery.desc: Visitors pause in their journey to take a break, take in the scenery, and read more at this overlook. Location: Maine (44.406° N 68.236° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by Dennis Adams
